title: Ports, Immutable Domain Values, Promise Ownership, and Lifecycle · Type-System Trace: Feed Ts Abort Tree
summary: Modules import global pools and fetch directly, promises float, AbortSignals are replaced, mutable objects cross layers, and shutdown cannot enumerate active handles. Predict the reader decision, external-unknown boundary, Promise and AbortSignal ownership, PostgreSQL transaction, failure, and observable evidence before reading the governing source. Planned artifact: Add and verify the feed ts abort tree increment in explicitly owned TypeScript service graph. Learners progress through predictio…
objectives: Compose caller, timeout, shutdown, and job-ownership signals without losing the abort reason or leaking listeners.

Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove directed ports, immutable admission, Promise ownership, AbortSignal trees, and handle closure; the release claim must bind source, compiler, emitted JavaScript, migrations, lockfile, package, image, configuration, and restored state; then change one PostgreSQL constraint, transaction, migration, upsert, query, or search plan and preserve admitted state. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original RSS, Atom, HTTP, PostgreSQL, and reader fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, fast-xml-parser, Express, PostgreSQL, processes, signals, handle and load claims, containers, accessibility review, backups, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Compose caller, timeout, shutdown, and job-ownership signals without losing the abort reason or leaking listeners.

For Feed aggregation and TypeScript Defend the Feed Ts Abort Tree model · Theory Feed Ts Abort Tree, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: One global AbortController is sufficient for every request and worker.
